summary.catchwords: FAMILY LAW – PROPERTY – Where litigation funding by way of partial property settlement is sought – Where $1.7 million is held upon trust for the parties in a solicitor’s trust account – Where the order sought does not exceed the applicant’s entitlement to property settlement – Where it is just and equitable to make an order for a partial property settlement – Where a partial property settlement in the sum of $200,000 is appropriate.

fulltext:
REASONS FOR JUDGMENT

STRUM J:

On 8 December 2025, the husband filed a Response to an Application in a Proceeding seeking, inter alia, litigation funding in the sum of $200,000, by way of partial property settlement, pursuant to s 79 and s 80 of the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth) (“Act”). That application is opposed by the wife. The husband relies upon an affidavit filed on 6 March 2026, and the wife relies upon an affidavit filed by my leave this day.

LEGAL PRINCIPLES

The principles applicable to the Application are well settled. In Strahan & Strahan (Interim Property Orders) [2011] FamCAFC 126; (2011) FLC 93-466 at [118], the Full Court said:
... There are two stages to the hearing of such an application where the power is to be exercised pursuant to s 80(1)(h) of the Act. This is recognised by the fact that although the power under s 79 should ordinarily be exercised on a once only basis, “circumstances may arise before there can be a final hearing” where the power is exercised. Thus the first step is to resolve whether to exercise the power before a final hearing and if it is resolved to do so then the second step involves the exercise of that power.
The Full Court earlier said at [115]:
The first consideration is the adjectival or procedural step and the second consideration is the substantive step. The first step requires consideration as to whether the jurisdiction will be entertained. The second step arises if the jurisdiction is entertained and requires consideration of the factors which are relevant to the exercise of power under s 79 to make an order.
The Full Court continued at [132]:
In relation to the first stage, in our view, when considering whether to exercise the power under s 79 and s 80(1)(h) of the Act to make an interim property order the “overarching consideration” is the interests of justice. It is not necessary to establish compelling circumstances. All that is required is that in the circumstances it is appropriate to exercise the power. In exercising the wide and unfettered discretion conferred by the power to make such an order, regard should be had to the fact that the usual order pursuant to s 79 is a once and for all order made after a final hearing.
The Full Court said at [135]:
In relation to the second matter, as the jurisdiction under s 79 of the Act is being exercised the provisions of that section must be considered and applied but with limitations given that it is not the final hearing. There is also no requirement of compelling circumstances in relation to the substantive step.
At [136], the Full Court referred to an issue that was referred to by both counsel in this matter. There, the Full Court said:
As to the third matter identified at 79,930 by the Full Court in Harris, in discussion before us it was described as the “adjustment issue” or “claw-back issue”. It was submitted by senior counsel for the Wife that it is relevant to consider whether an order would give the applicant “more than they would be indubitably entitled to on a final hearing” or alternatively “would it give them so much that it could not be adjusted on a final hearing?” As we have observed the Full Court in Zschokke at 83,220-221 stressed the importance of consideration of the “adjustment issue” if the power in s 80(1)(h) of the Act is being exercised. We accept the submission and observe that this matter is relevant because the discretion conferred by the power in s 79 is to make such order as the Court considers appropriate provided it is just and equitable to make the order in circumstances where the power will not be exhausted by the interim order. As Bryant CJ and Coleman J observed in Gabel v Yardley at [69] and [72] the interim order must be capable of variation or reversal without resort to s 79A of the Act or appeal. As Finn J said at [126] the interim order must be “capable of alteration at any time prior to, or as part of, the final exercise of the s 79 power”.
Earlier in its judgment, the Full Court said at [113]:
There is only one exercise of the power under s 79 of the Act. However, this power may “be exercised by a succession of orders until the power ... is exhausted” and the power is exhausted “when there remains no property ... with respect to which orders by way of alteration of interests in property could be or have been made.”: Gabel v Yardley per Bryant CJ and Coleman J at [57]. As Finn J in Gabel v Yardley at [125] said: “it is only the final order, which deals on a final basis with all known property of the parties, which completes the one single exercise of the s 79 power”. Further, an earlier order whether made under s 79(6) or s 80(1)(h) is capable of alteration at any time prior to, or as part of the final exercise of the s 79 power: Gabel v Yardley per Bryant CJ and Coleman J at [69]–[73] and Finn J at [126].
This leads to consideration of what the Full Court subsequently said in Shinohara & Shinohara [2025] FedCFamC1A 126. At [101], reference was made to Trevi & Trevi [2018] FamCAFC 173; (2018) FLC 93-858, saying that the Full Court in that case:
described the propositions underscoring the notional adding back of property that did not exist to existing interests in property as “unduly simplistic” and that justice and equity can be achieved by taking up the consideration of the use of property that no longer exists as a relevant s 75(2) factor, the latter course being “perhaps, technically more correct”. Notwithstanding that Full Court guidance, the conceptually dubious practice of notional add backs generally continued unabated.
The Full Court in Shinohara at [124] considered the subsequent amendments to the Act, and the inclusion of what were once referred to as “s 75(2) factors” into s 79(5), being “[c]onsiderations relating to current and future circumstances”. The Full Court, relevantly for present purposes, said:
... Section 79(5)(n) directs consideration to the extent to which each party has contributed to the property or financial resources of the other party at the time of the trial and prospectively. This could encompass interim or partial property adjustments or payments by way of litigation funding. Section 79(5)(v) replicates s 75(2)(o) prior to the amendment of the section. Longstanding authority has not read down the scope of the considerations falling within that sub-section, described in the Explanatory Memorandum as a “catch all’ provision, including but not limited to enabling the current and future considerations of the fact and impact of property that has been the subject of predisposition prior to hearing, being a category of notional add back identified in Townsend and Omacini, or the expenditure of property on legal fees.
The Full Court continued at [125]:
So that it is clear, s 79 now directs that the categories identified in Omacini pre-amendment that were notionally added back are to be considered in ensuring a just and equitable outcome, either by way of historical contributions, or by way of their relationship to and impact upon the current and future circumstances at the s 79(5) stage. ...
Lest there be any concerns about the quantum sought by the husband and how it could be treated pursuant to s 79(5)(n) or s 79(5)(v) at trial, I note that in Waters & Jurek [1995] FamCA 101; [1995] FLC 92-635, the Full Court referred to the trial judge having adjusted, in the wife’s favour, the conclusions he had reached on contributions by an additional $50,000, pursuant to s 75(2). Baker J said of that, at 82,388, as follows:
The trial Judge determined that because of the disparity in income, in respect of which there was really no dispute, there should be an adjustment of $50,000 in favour of the respondent. Such an approach is not only consistent with authority, but is expressly provided for in the section itself. I am not therefore persuaded that any appellable error has been demonstrated.
In relation to a s 75(2) adjustment, the Full Court in Docters van Leeuwen & Docters van Leeuwen [1990] FLC 92-148 at 78,025 said:
No doubt cases will occur in which an order for the payment of a fixed sum without conversion into a percentage figure will be appropriate. ...
It is therefore apparent from the dicta of the Full Court in Docters van Leeuwen and Waters & Jurek that whilst in many, if not most, cases adjustments under s 79(5) are made on a percentage basis, they may also be made on a dollar basis.

APPLICATION

I turn, therefore, to the first or adjectival step, namely, whether the power under s 79 should be exercised at this interlocutory juncture. The husband deposes, and the wife does not dispute, that the net value of the asset pool is in the order of $4.8 million. This includes the sum of approximately $1.7 million held by the wife’s solicitors upon trust for the parties, being the balance of the proceeds of sale of a real property. The husband deposes to estimated future legal costs in the order of $179,000, and the reasonableness of that amount is not disputed by the wife. She suggests that he resort to litigation funding, as she has done. In circumstances where there are substantial liquid funds available to the parties, there is no reason, in principle, why either should have to have recourse to litigation funding.
The husband does not propose, nor does the wife seek, that she should receive a similar amount. However, it is clear from his affidavit that he does not oppose such a payment to her. If she does, that may either be resolved consensually between them or by the Court. However, for the reasons above, in the circumstances of this case, I find that it is just and equitable to make an order for a partial property settlement at this juncture in the proceedings.
I turn, therefore, to the second or substantive step, namely, the quantum of the order to be made. The husband deposes that the net pool is in the order of $4.8 million net, and that is not disputed by the wife. She deposes that the husband has withdrawn and/or had the benefit of over $700,000, but does not say by how much more. I infer that it is not more than $800,000 (or even, for example, $750,000 or $725,000) because, if that were the case, she would have said so. Accordingly, I shall proceed on her estimate of a figure in the order of $700,000.
That is not expressly disputed by the husband; however, I take into account the fact that he only received the wife’s affidavit late yesterday, in circumstances where it was ordered to be filed a week ago. Accordingly, I do not take him to admit that he has had the benefit of $700,000, or in the order thereof. I take the wife’s case at its highest, but noting what the Full Court said in Shinohara, regarding so-called “add-backs”. Whilst the wife’s affidavit is comprised of other amounts, her affidavit is opaque, and her counsel was unable to shed any light on those amounts. Approximately nine pages of her affidavit, totalling 16 pages, is replete with tables of debits and credits, but what they mean, and what they amount to, if anything, is presently unascertainable.
The husband submits, and the wife cannot deny, that taking her evidence at its highest, if he receives the sum of $200,000, as he seeks, in addition to the total sum of $700,000 she deposes he has had, the total amount of $900,000 will equate to approximately 18.75 per cent of a pool of approximately $4.8 million. That depends upon how the monies allegedly had and received are treated pursuant to s 79(5); it may amount to less than that. Nowhere does the wife particularise what she proposes the husband should receive by way of overall property settlement nor, indeed, how her ss 79(4)(a), 79(4)(b) and 79(4)(c) contributions and other s 79(4) and s 79(5) factors should be assessed. Both her amended initiating application, filed on 5 June 2024, and her affidavit, filed this day, are silent in this regard. She does not assert that, let alone explain how, his entitlement could be less than 18.75 per cent, however assessed and calculated.
At its highest, at paragraph 22 of her affidavit, having deposed to the husband having withdrawn, or had the benefit of, over $700,000, she deposes that she is:
... concerned that, if he receives an additional part of property settlement, this may be beyond his entitlement to a property settlement.
The husband, in his affidavit, concedes that both the wife and he contributed to the acquisition, conservation and improvement of their property. He deposes that both the wife and he received gifts from their parents. For example, in 2011, he established F Pty Ltd with an initial investment of approximately $250,000, gifted by his parents, which he used to open his first retail business. This is not denied by the wife in her affidavit.
He refers to a property in C Street, Suburb E, that was purchased in 2009 for $659,000. He concedes that the wife’s mother contributed the sum of $375,130 towards the purchase of this property, whilst the balance was obtained by way of a mortgage loan. He deposes that, during the relationship, he contributed towards the mortgage repayments for the property, and other household expenses and repairs. He deposes that the C Street mortgage was refinanced in the wife’s sole name in 2013, in order to redevelop the property by subdividing it into two townhouses. He deposes that his father and he personally carried out landscaping work for the properties, one of which was sold for just over $1 million in February 2015.
Insofar as s 79(5) factors are concerned, the husband asserts that both the wife and he are in good health and that, at trial, he will be seeking an adjustment, inter alia, on the basis of current and future needs occasioned by the children being in his care. However, it appears that, for most of their lives, the children, who are 12 and 10 years of age, have lived with and/or been cared for by the husband’s parents. There is an extant interim order that the children live with his parents and spend time with the husband by agreement. There is a recommendation in the most recent Family Report that they not spend any time with the mother at present or, if they do, that it be supervised.
The wife, similarly, deposes to the contributions she asserts were made by her, or on her behalf, at paragraphs 10–26 of her affidavit. Unlike the husband, she does not concede that her parents-in-law made any contributions. I similarly take all of those matters into account. I cannot presently make any findings in relation to issues in contest.
However, as referred to earlier, I also take into account the fact that the children have not lived with the mother for a substantial period of time and are presently in the care of the husband’s parents. I have perused the two Family Reports, from December 2023 and February 2026. It appears not to be contested that the children are not presently even spending time with the mother, and that they have not done so for approximately the past six months. It may be that, at trial, her s 79(4)(c) contributions and her s 79(5) factors are less than might otherwise be the case.

DISPOSITION

I take into account the matters to which I have referred, including the s 79(4) contributions asserted by each of the parties and, in particular, those of the husband, as the applicant for a partial property settlement; the s 79(5) factors that may apply to the parties; and that the wife is presently unable to satisfy me, on balance, that the quantum of the partial property settlement sought by the husband might exceed his entitlements at trial.
In the circumstances, I am satisfied both that it is presently just and equitable to make an order for a partial property settlement and that it is just and equitable and appropriate to do so in the quantum sought by the husband.
